sources.review: Bergsvik, Fauske & Hart - 'Can Policies Stall the Fertility Fall? A Systematic Review of the (Quasi-)Experimental Literature'
sources.where: Verbatim search strings published in the companion protocol (Fauske, Hart & Bergsvik 2020, SocArXiv preprint t8vsg, https://osf.io/preprints/socarxiv/t8vsg), Appendices 1-4 (per-database strings for Epistemonikos, Cochrane, Ovid MEDLINE, Web of Science, RePEc/EconPapers). The fertility-outcome cluster is: (birth-rate* OR fertility-rate* OR "birth rate" OR "fertility rate" OR "completed fertility" OR "parity progression" OR "total fertility rate" OR "cohort fertility rate" OR "lifetime fertility"), plus '*fertility*' and the Cochrane form ((birth or fertilit*) NEXT rate*) and MeSH 'Birth Rate'.
sources.notes: Primary, fully verbatim source for the fertility block. Its policy terms cover leave/childcare/transfers (parental leave, cash transfers, cash allowances, tax*, lump-sum, cash-for-care) and housing - NO pension/old-age terms, as anticipated.

sources.review: Thomas, Rowe, Williamson & Lin - 'The effect of leave policies on increasing fertility: a systematic review'
sources.where: Humanities and Social Sciences Communications 9:262. Full per-database Boolean string is in Supplementary Appendix B (search procedure for 'Academic Search Complete'); not reproduced in main text (only retrieved as confirmatory). Main text + Fig.1 confirm the fertility/childbearing concept and 16-database search (Scopus, WoS, EconLit, Medline, PsycINFO, Sociological Abstracts, etc.).
sources.notes: Confirms 'childbearing'/'fertility' as the outcome concept across a large demographic-economics database set; verbatim wildcard list sits in the supplement.

sources.review: Perera et al. - 'Impact of social protection on gender equality in low- and middle-income countries: A systematic review of reviews' (Campbell Collaboration)
sources.where: Verbatim search strategy in the published protocol (Perera et al. 2021, Campbell Systematic Reviews; PMC8356277), Appendix A, Academic Search Complete (run 23/02/2021), social-protection concept group. Term list (verbatim): "Social assistance", "Social Pension*", "Public Pension", "Pension*", "Old Age Assistance", "Old Age Pension", "In-kind Transfer*", "Conditional Cash Transfer", "Unconditional Cash Transfer", "Cash Transfer*", "Social Transfer", "Income Maintenance", "Income Support", "Social grant", "Social Safety Net", "Non-contributory", "Contributory", "Annuities", "Health Insurance", "National Insurance", "Social Insurance", "Social Security", "Social Welfare", "Death Benefit", "Disability Grant*", "Unemployment Benefit*" (plus leave/childcare/voucher/housing terms not carried over here).
sources.notes: PRIMARY verbatim source for the pension/old-age-security block. A published systematic-review search string, so leakage-free, even though the review's outcome is gender equality (not fertility) - it is being used here only as external controlled vocabulary for the pension/social-protection concept.

sources.review: Tomaz et al. - 'Associations between retirement, social security policies and the health of older people: a systematic review'
sources.where: BMC Public Health. Methods + Table 1. Indexed search used ("social security" AND "aging") across Embase, WoS, Scopus, PubMed, CINAHL, ASSIA, APA PsycNet; PROSPERO CRD42021225820. PICO intervention defined verbatim as 'retirement guarantees ... pension benefits, health insurance, subsidized assistance and other contributory schemes'.
sources.notes: Confirms 'social security', 'retirement', 'pension benefits', 'health insurance', 'contributory schemes' as concept vocabulary. The executed Boolean string itself is thin (just 'social security' AND 'aging').

sources.review: Old-age-security-motive empirical literature (author keywords + JEL controlled vocabulary)
sources.where: Willis (1980) NBER w0372 'The Old Age Security Hypothesis and Population Growth' (names the hypothesis: 'old age security'); social-pension/fertility quasi-experimental paper on Namibia (RePEc gat/wpaper/1933; CEPREMAP docweb2002) with author keywords verbatim: 'Fertility; Old-age pensions; Social security; Africa; Difference-in-differences'; JEL H55 = 'Social Security and Public Pensions', J13 = 'Fertility'.
sources.notes: Supplies the OAS-theory naming ('old age security', 'old-age pensions') and the canonical fertility x social-security intersection vocabulary as standard controlled terminology, since no dedicated OAS-pension-fertility systematic review exists.

notes: FERTILITY block is well-sourced and largely verbatim: every term except 'childbear*' and 'number of children' is taken directly from the Bergsvik/Fauske 2020 protocol search strings (Appendices 1-4); 'childbear*'/'number of children' are added as standard demographic controlled vocabulary (childbearing is the outcome concept named in Thomas et al. 2022 and across demography). PENSION/OAS block is THINNER and more vocabulary-driven: there is NO dedicated systematic review of pension/social-security effects on fertility or of the 'children as old-age security' motive, so it has no purpose-built published string. The block is therefore assembled from (a) a verbatim published social-protection review search string (Perera et al. 2021 protocol, Appendix A) - the strongest external anchor, (b) a retirement/social-security health review (Tomaz et al. 2024), and (c) author keywords + JEL controlled vocabulary from the OAS-motive empirical literature (Willis 1980; Namibia social-pension study). Terms 'provident fund', 'superannuation', 'old-age support', and 'old[- ]age security' are standard international social-security terminology (ILO/SSA usage) included to cover non-Western pension systems and the theoretical motive; they are NOT from a single published string but are defensible controlled vocabulary. Leakage-free guarantee holds: none of these terms derive from our own labeled gold papers - all come from other authors' published methods sections, registered protocols, or standard JEL/MeSH/ILO vocabulary.
